groups.name: The Wire Universe
groups.explanation: Jimmy McNulty (character), Stringer Bell (character), David Simon (showrunner/creator), and Idris Elba (actor who played Stringer Bell) are all central to The Wire.

groups.name: TV Shows Remade Shot-for-Shot (or Near-Identical) in Another Country
groups.explanation: Broadchurch (UK) was remade as Gracepoint (US) with the same lead actor David Tennant. The Killing is an American remake of the Danish series Forbrydelsen. Carrie Mathison is the lead of Homeland, which is a remake of the Israeli series Prisoners of War (Hatufim). All four tiles are directly tied to English-language TV shows that are near-identical remakes of foreign originals.

groups.name: TV Showrunners Who Left a Long-Running Show Mid-Series (And Were Replaced)
groups.explanation: Shonda Rhimes stepped back from Grey's Anatomy mid-run. Alan Ball left True Blood after season 5 before its conclusion. Amy Poehler (as star/producer) saw Parks and Recreation's showrunner Michael Schur reduce his role mid-series. Springfield (The Simpsons) saw its original showrunner Matt Groening's creative partners cycle through multiple replacements over the show's decades-long run — all four are associated with shows where the original creative lead departed before the series ended.

groups.name: TV Characters Who Conceal a Disfigurement or Prosthetic Under Their Clothing or Mask
groups.explanation: Richard Harrow (Boardwalk Empire) wears a tin mask to hide his disfigured face. Brenda Leigh Johnson (The Closer) conceals a shopping addiction tied to deep psychological wounds — a hidden, masked inner life. The Peach Pit (Beverly Hills 90210) is where Nat, who had a stroke, hid his disability behind the counter for much of the series. Pawnee, Indiana (Parks and Recreation) is home to Ron Swanson, who maintains an elaborate false public persona (the anti-government libertarian) as a mask over his true competence — players expect these to be a 'fictional towns/locations' group, but all four are associated with a major recurring theme of concealment of a physical or deeply personal hidden truth behind a literal or figurative mask.
